content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 19, 1990


Don Benninghoven
Executive Director
League of California Cities
1400 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. A-90-411

Dear Mr. Benninghoven:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Iola Williams, who is the President of the League of
California Cities and a San Francisco City Councilmember, and on behalf
of other elected officials who are members of the League of
California Cities. You have asked for advice concerning
Councilmember Williams' responsibilities as an elected official
under the new "personal use" provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1/

     This letter confirms the telephone advice provided to Ms.
JoAnne Speers of your office on June 22, 1990. Please be aware
that the issues raised in this letter present significant policy
questions and therefore will be presented to the Commission for
consideration in the future in the form of a regulation. In the
meantime, we have provided a conservative and cautious
interpretation of the Act.

     Effective January 1, 1990, the Act was amended by Senate Bill
1431 to include new provisions that regulate the appropriate use
of campaign funds. (Section 85800, et seq.) The purpose of the
legislation was "to ensure that candidates, elected officers and
the people clearly recognize acceptable and unacceptable uses of
campaign funds." (Senate Elections Committee Analysis of SB
1431.)

     The general rule of the new personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably

1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-411
Page 2

related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 85801.) However, where an expenditure confers a
substantial personal benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the
expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) A substantial personal
benefit is defined as a direct personal benefit to the candidate
or officeholder of $100 or more. (Section 85800(b)(2).)

Moreover, the new personal use law sets forth provisions
dealing with specific types of expenditures. Section 85802(a)(1),
which deals with travel, provides:

(a) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay
or reimburse the candidate, the elected officer, or
employees or staff of the campaign committee or the
elected officer's governmental agency for travel
expenses and necessary accommodations except when
these expenditures are directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

(1) For purposes of this section, payments or
reimbursements for travel and necessary accommodations shall be considered as directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose if
the payments would meet standards similar to the
standards of the Internal Revenue Service pursuant
to Sections 162 and 274 of the Internal Revenue
Code for deductions of travel expenses under the
federal income tax law.

(2) For the purposes of this section,
payments or reimbursement for travel by the
household of a candidate or elected officer when
traveling to the same destination in order to
accompany the candidate or elected officer shall be
considered for the same purpose as the candidate's
or elected officer's travel.
(Emphasis added.)

According to Section 85802(a)(1), the use of campaign funds
for payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary accommodations is permissible if the payments meet standards similar to the
standards for the deduction of travel expenses pursuant to
Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and 274.

Our File No. A-90-411
Page 3

As was discussed with Ms. Spears of your office, in the near
future the Commission will begin considering regulations
interpreting this section. Thus, it is possible that the
Commission may adopt regulations which may be at variance with the
advice given in this letter. However, until the Commission adopts
regulations, we are interpreting Section 85302(a)(1) to require a
political, legislative or governmental purpose for the travel and
accommodations related either to the elective office held or the
elective office sought by the official. If there is a political,
legislative or governmental purpose, campaign funds may be used
for ordinary and necessary expenses associated with the travel and
accommodation consistent with the Internal Revenue Service
standards.2/

Internal Revenue Code Section 162(a)(2) sets forth general
rules which permit the deduction of all ordinary and necessary
travel expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade
or business. In light of the purpose behind the new personal use
law, we have interpreted the Internal Revenue Code Sections to
allow only for the use of campaign funds for the "business
portions" of the trip. Thus, the 14-day trip to Europe may be
broken into business days (days on which there are meetings) and
nonbusiness days.3/ Where a weekend falls between business days,
the weekend will also be counted as business time. By our
calculations nine-fourteenths of the fee for the trip may be paid
from the campaign funds of elected officials.4/

_________________________

2/ You stated in your letter and Ms. Spears confirmed in our
telephone conversation of June 22, 1990 that the city councils of
the various city officials who wish to go on the trip will be
considering the relationship between the trip and a governmental
purpose related to the official's office. Since we have not yet
defined a "governmental purpose" under the personal use sections,
we will rely on the local agency's resolution that the trip is
directly related to a governmental purpose. However, please note,
that the definition of "political, legislative or governmental
purpose" may be defined in a regulation in the future.

3/ Your letter stated that officials may plan other meetings on
days that are not already scheduled for meetings in the itinerary.
Any such meetings are speculative at this time. Absent more
information we would conservatively conclude that the potential of
other meetings would not change the results of this letter.

4/ The same rules apply to the spouse of an elected official.
Section 85802(a)(2) provides: "For the purposes of this section,
payments or reimbursement for travel by the household of a
candidate or elected officer when traveling to the same
destination in order to accompany the candidate or elected officer
shall be considered for the same purpose as the candidate's or
elected officer's travel." Thus, the pro rated amount of the
spouses fee may also be paid with campaign funds.

Our File No. A-90-411
Page 4

However, no part of expenditures for solely personal
purposes, such as entertainment or sight-seeing, may be paid for
with campaign funds. Consequently, since these personal expenses
appear to be part of the "package price," they must be subtracted
out of the total fee prior to the proration of the fee.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:dg
